title: add support for squashing all-but-base-image to buildah
body:
### Issue Description

Buildah supports one type of squashing only with `--squash`: it outputs a container image consisting of one layer, squashing the base image and all build steps.

By contrast, Podman supports `--squash-all`, corresponding to buildah's behaviour, and `--squash`, which squashes all the _new_ layers but does not flatten that into the base image (or above). I think this might match the behaviour of Docker, but I'm not sure, I haven't used Docker's squashing features.

Squash-all-new-layers, whilst layering on top of the specified base image, is very useful behaviour. It ensures that layer and image caching between common base images continues to work; it separates concerns and provides a clean provenance boundary.

I produce a container downstream of Red Hat UBI and the build/release pipeline we use has a test "BasedOnUbi" that relies upon the presence of the Base image (UBI) layers in order to detect that the image being built is conformant: buildah's squashing behaviour breaks the test. I need to either get special permission to waive the test, or ship an unsquashed image, which will be worse for users.

(It's unfortunate the naming of these options is inconsistent.)

### Steps to reproduce the issue

n/a

### Describe the results you received

n/a

```toml
driver = "overlay"
runroot = "/run/containers/storage"

[storage.options]
additionalimagestores = [

mountopt = "nodev"
```

### Additional information

comments.body: My understanding is that the behavior you're describing is the default, i.e. "--layers=false", and it wasn't even an option in early releases, it was the only way it worked.  Support for creating an intermediate image at each step ("--layers=true") was added later, and the original behavior was left as the default.  When podman came along, its default for `podman build` was set it to true, to better match what `docker build` does.

@nalind , you are absolutely right. I'd ignored the `--layers` argument, incorrectly believing it to be related to layer caching, and not impacting the composition of the final image. The default of `--layers[=false]` and not specifying `--squash` does seem to be exactly what I want.
